speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to read it? No, just read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. December 11, 1995, Alberta Szalita, and interviewer, Greta Elbogen, New York City, USA, English language. Sure. My name is Greta Elbogen. The date is December 11, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Dr. Alberta Szalita in New York City in the United States. Dr. Szalita, can you tell us your name and its spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My given name was Brandla. The spelling is B-R-A-N-D-L-A. I was born in a small town. It was what they call a shtetl, [RUSSIAN] in-- in Russian. And I remember myself from the age, I would say, about three and a half, four. I lived-- the, the, the shtetl was divided so that you had a couple of streets where Jews lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mind telling us the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country at that time, the shtetl-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the shtetl was divided so that around the main streets where the Jews lived and a few non-Jews, it was-- there, there were Ukrainian peasants around in small, little huts, you can call them, little houses. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a Jewish family. And we had servants in the house which were non-Jews. Like, for-- the girl for the children was a, a German woman. For cleaning were Ukrainians-- a woman cleaner. And then a man was busying himself with heating, you know, that water.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of house did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very beautiful house which was standing in the center of the city. To, to the left side was a Russian Greek Orthodox Church. And farther, farther there, behind it, was a Catholic church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Did you belong to any synagogue at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents were going to the synagogue. When holidays were there, my mother also went to the synagogue. I never went to the synagogue. There was a main synagogue which my father-- well, my father's father, my grandfather, gave money to build the building. And in addition, we're what they called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the name of that synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called the, the shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: And beside there were little shtiebels, they called them, which were for Hasidic-- different Hasidic, like Trisk Rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH] Rabbi, we lived-- we lived under that-- where there were those synagogues. I started to be taught at home when I was four years old, close to four years old. My mother was teaching me Russian. And I had a teacher coming to teach me Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearby-- near our house, to, to the left, let us say, from the veranda was an aunt, one that lived there, Mume Chaia, we used to call her. Aizenberg was her name-- family name. And my father considered that girls should also know and understand what they are reading. So it was not that you learned-- the-- I was taught to read. But I was taught to understand what the words mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many sisters, brothers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had one brother who was older than myself. And then I was the next. I was the second child. And then there were-- I had sisters. At the time that I'm talking about, already two of them who were born. And pretty soon-- maybe since when I remember-- my next sister was born. So three younger sisters were born a year, a year and a half apart. The youngest sister was born in 1925. But she was born in Volodymyr-Volynsky. That's where I went to gymnasium. But that was not at the time that we are talking about. In that shtetl-- if it-- since you were interested in the Jewish aspects of it-- I never knew that there was something wrong to being, being a Jew. On the contrary, the idea was that the non-Jews were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inferior. I-- I remember that when I went to school in Rostov-- later on you'll hear about it-- I was surprised that a non-Jew was teaching me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. You mean, maybe, in your town there was a majority of Jewish people?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason? You think there was a majority of Jews in your town--

speaker: interviewee
text: The most of it were then, then the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the non-Jews, as they say in my house, were, were dust itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one day I was sitting and listening to-- my mother had a visitor that was the wife of the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is the head of the police. And this woman, the [NON-ENGLISH]'s wife, she was asking my mother how come the Jews call a, a boy an [NON-ENGLISH], and the non-Jew, they call a shegetz?

speaker: interviewee
text: How come? And how come when a Jew dies, you have a funeral-- a levaya? When a non-Jew dies, they say, [NON-ENGLISH], if you understand the words.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was like the discrimination was in this favor of the non-Jews. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived in this town till the-- till the, the First World War. When the First World War broke out, pretty soon the front line was getting closer to the place where I lived. So then my parents decided to leave the place, to go to the depth of Russia. And we left, went to-- wound up in the Rostov-on-the-Don. I don't know whether you know about the city Rostov-- R-O-S-T-O--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that-- right now, it's already also on the border of Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, it was czarist Russia. And that's-- so that's where went. We went.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember a fire when I was a child. That was very, very early. That's why I say somewhere around three, three and a half, there was a fire. And I remember standing on this place which was in front of our house, and my mother holding my hand, and people were throwing out beddings and whatever. And the fire was like a-- like a tongue of fire going over the roofs.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about somebody else's house, not your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and then a new house was built. So that when we left-- this First World War when we left, it was a new house, a beautiful, big house. And I understood from some of my relatives that the architect was somebody from Petrograd. At that time it was Petersburg. And my father built that house with great interest and, and spent an awful lot of money on-- on this house. And he-- my father was only son of a rich lumber merchant. And he was educated like the Russian nobility were educated, you know, where teachers were imported to teach him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandfather died when I was very small. I just have an image of him, not anything else. Apparently, he loved me very much. But as I say, that's all I know him. And he was known as [NON-ENGLISH], if you know the word-- the rich man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rich man. What did your father do at this time? How did he earn his--

speaker: interviewee
text: At, at, at this time, the way I look at it now, he must have been just spending money that his father left him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Did your life change when you moved over?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be changing as we go on. So we wound up early in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Rostov-on-the-Don, rented an apartment there on Romanovskaya Ulica. That was-- the Romanov were the czarist family. And that was the name of the street. When we came to, to Rostov, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I cannot say either way, but pretty soon, I started to hear about children teasing, teasing Jews like-- [NON-ENGLISH], whatever. Some you, you heard. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is-- that says the name 'Chaia' is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So that started to have an inkling that there are Jewish and non-Jewish. And of course, the main major population was non-Jews. It was a completely-- a complete difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: I then was close to six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What kind of school did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then, then they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they started to, to teach us Russian in the school. So came first to a-- to a school. We worked with-- the teacher asked me my name. And I said Brandla. So she said, why don't we call you Berta?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how gradually my name from Brandla became Berta and afterwards became Alberta.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Originally you only had Brandla.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's how they called me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in-- in this little town, the life was kosher, a Jewish life. Yiddish was spoken all around. Except my parents, with each other, they spoke Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother finished gymnasium in Luck. So she was educated officially, formally. In other words, she went to gymnasium. My father was taught in that-- in that shtetl by teachers, special teachers-- was very well educated, but without going to any school. In, in Rostov, it was where my father was occupied with my brother's education. And my mother was entrusted with the education of the children. That's how my parents agreed to. My mother wanted me to go to gymnasium. And she took me one day. And I was going to a school where they was teaching me Russian-- and write and read. We took me to gymnasium. And at that time, in order to be accepted to the second preparatory school before the first class of gymnasium, you have to-- you had to know this. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It means Russian words that were used in [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH], that is now not used anymore. After the Revolution, it was changed. But you had to know a list of words. The, the root of these words were in-- you had to learn them by heart to know how to spell. This was a spelling--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was changed at the time. So-- and I had to know the multiplication table.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember I was standing there. My mother was with a long skirt. And the teacher was standing with a long skirt, and I between them. I didn't know exactly what they were talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother-- let us say it was a Thursday. And, and the teacher said that I cannot get to-- to that class without knowing the multiplication table and the-- and the root words with a [NON-ENGLISH]. So my mother said, she will know it by Monday. And she sat down with me. And Monday, I did-- yeah, I knew the table of multiplication. And I knew this list. And I started to go to the gymnasium. In that gymnasium in the morning, all the girls from around one to eight class and the  three preparatory classes went in, in the upper floor. There was a hall where they had a, a Moleben, it was called. Because it means 'prayers.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was like a, a Catholic school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a Greek Orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: Greek Orthodox. And so we all went in the morning. And I was the only girl that was standing. Because they were going on their knees at a certain point. And I was standing like this, like a mushroom, listening to those prayers. And there was a [NON-ENGLISH]-- means a priest-- and an icon in the corner. So I was exposed to that from the very beginning. And I was listening to the prayers. And I still remember those prayers. But my father at that time insisted that I wouldn't go on Saturday. And they thought, maybe, I was a rabbi's daughter or something like that. But nobody was teasing me, nothing. Just it was a strange world.

speaker: interviewee
text: An interesting world, but a strange world. Well, that's how I was exposed to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think you were the only Jew in that gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I didn't see-- I didn't see others standing. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you have friends there? Or how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty soon-- pretty soon, I was a very good pupil. And people treated me well. And, and I was not-- I don't remember anything, except that it was like entering a world that you had very little knowledge of. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In, in the, the house where we lived, there were a lot of-- it was a big house. And there were a lot of tenants. And we pretty soon started to know the children there and played together. But as I said, that's when I started to hear the teasing of Jews and, well, entered into my life, let us say, much more an understanding of a Jewish girl in a non-Jewish world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your sisters go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a year later-- and I cannot even say exactly. Because I went myself to that school. Another school that I-- after I jumped over the classes, I mean, I studied in summertime and had to change a school in order to enter a higher grade where-- [INAUDIBLE] some Jewish girls there in that-- in those schools. And then one of my sisters, Sarah, was going to the same school beside it. And subsequently, I cannot tell you much about their schooling. I can say much more about my own. But again, I, I, I wouldn't be able to go in any details about what, what happened to them. I know only one thing, that we used to go to schools. My mother used, used to sit and do lessons with us. And friends, friends of those-- my sisters were also coming sometimes to the same home. And my mother was helping them, too. She liked to teach the children. And she busied herself with this-- what they-- what they were doing. My father at that time-- we had a-- we had a cousin that lived in the same-- Rostov. They came-- their family came from Brest [NON-ENGLISH], if you are familiar with that name. And they lived at the one part of the city. And we lived in the center of the city. And they had four boys and one girl. And we had four girls and one boy. And more or less, they were parallel, parallel ages. It's like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a cousin-- he recently died. He died in Israel. Chlawne was his name-- Chlawne, C-H-L-A-W-N-E. He was approximately my age. And subsequently, I could enumerate their names and their ages. So it was like a, a correspondence-- parallel. Well, they didn't go to gymnasium. But we-- it was my mother's doing. We all went to gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandparents? Have you known them--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents-- my mother's-- my mother's parents, I knew. My grandmother died a normal life-- a normal, like, death. And my grandfather was killed by the Germans in, in Luck in 1942, together with the rest of my family. But them, I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when did I get to know them? My grandmother once came to, to visit us in Wlodzimierzec. She brought me gift, I remember. She brought me a bracelet. And she brought gifts to all the children. So her, I remember from that early life.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the grandfather-- him, my mother's father, I got to know later when we returned back to-- from Rostov, back home. That happened in 1922, close to 1923. Now the life in Rostov, which was from 1916 till '22, was already a turbulent life. Initially, my father opened what was called [NON-ENGLISH]. They were sending out packages, just packing up and sending away things. It was a kind of a bureau of, of transporting things. I don't know what you would--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Rostov to wherever in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, something like this. That was-- that-- what, what he was making a living there, in Rostov. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was an interesting life. We're in this quarter that-- what I was starting to say-- that where we lived on this Romanov [NON-ENGLISH]. It was on the corner of [NON-ENGLISH], it was called. So there was a, a factory of cigarettes. And they were making boxes-- in beautiful boxes with-- they're packing cigarettes. And we were playing with all those. We were getting those boxes. And so I started to go to movies, start a kind of big-city life, going to theater, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ballet. It was a big city, Rostov-- relatively. For me, it was a very big city, a beautiful city. We used to go to the gardens there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know in what to-- what actually-- to what kind of details to go in about it. But you, you developed like a, a city child. And life was OK. Until-- then came 1917, yeah. In the gymnasium, they changed the, the prayer from, from-- praying for the-- for the health of the czar changed to Christ loving army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember my father came home, and he was singing 'The Marseillaise'-- 'The Marseillaise,' this French--

speaker: interviewee
text: --French song because he was a liberal. And he was happy with the fact that there was a revolution at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember, at home-- oh yeah, and I had a Hebrew teacher that came home to teach me. So I continued with Hebrew that I started in the shtetl. I continued in home quite solidly, to translate from Hebrew to Russian and from Russian to Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father was playing chess with that Hebrew teacher. And I remember I-- one day-- didn't want to do the lesson because-- and my father had a look at the book. And he decided that, that it's too difficult for me, what the teacher wanted me to do. And the teacher said that he, he knew what he was telling me to do. So I can do it. So it ended up that for a while, I didn't have these Hebrew lessons. And they stopped playing chess.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one nice day, I decided I'll translate what the teacher was wanting-- wanted. Took the book, translated it, and then they became-- they got reconciled with each other, my teacher with my father. But for a while, I didn't have to have those Hebrew lessons, because I didn't-- I was not going to do that, that assignment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could-- I could tell you a lot of things about this period between [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this 1917 and 1922, when we went back home. Home, I mean, where we were-- where I was born. [SIGHS] And first came to, to-- to Rostov, came the Germans. Then there was the fight

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us when that was, between 1917 and '24?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about the 18th or something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was a fight going between the Whites and the-- and the Red in Rostov. And we had, at one time-- at one time, we had in our house-- we had a very large-- larger than my living room here-- a very long living room. And there were staying in, in our house in that living room, yeshiva students. Something-- they-- somebody helped them that my father didn't have to go to the army. And it was promised to give to those yeshiva students holidays in our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came for the big holidays. And they stayed till well after Easter. And I remember I learned a lot of Hebrew, how they were singing these [NON-ENGLISH] Shabbat-- Sabbath, Sabbath songs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course there were-- during the fights, there was a whole, whole difficult period. My mother went away to, to Caucasus. And she couldn't return. In the meantime, that-- it, it changed in Rostov. Instead of the White came the Red. And the Red-- we had a maid from Luck that my grandmother sent to, to our home. And she had a boyfriend. And then he must have been involved with it-- some Russian Red. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, what-- we called them Red. At any rate, from there, the Bolsheviks took over Rostov. The head of this [NON-ENGLISH]-- there was a, a, a general [NON-ENGLISH]. They came into our-- into our apartment. And they robbed whatever, whatever they-- they took away whatever they wanted. Now, the way it went was like this. Mother wasn't-- was not in town. She was away. And she didn't come yet back. My father was sick. He had the typhus-- exanthematic fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he about then? How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old was he at that time? If-- if it is, let us say, in 1919, 1919, and he was born in-- in 14 years earlier there, 19 and 14. So he must have been 33 or 34.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I and my brother, we were taking care of him. She-- that maid of ours was not, at at that particular evening, in the house. And my father didn't know what was going on with him because he had terribly high fever. But at that time, they, they decided that since they were telling that Budyonny gave permission to, to the soldiers to take whatever they want in this--
